content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMTVIISSION
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 958 12-0807
(916) 322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
December 24, 1998
Joan Garcia Munoz
Corcoran City Council
Post Office Box 663
Corcoran, California 93212
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-98-281
Dear Ms. Munoz:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ Since your request does not seek advice
regarding any specific governmental decisions, and your questions are general in nature, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).
2
Please keep in mind that the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when issuing
advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Our advice is applicable only to the extent that
the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the material facts have been provided. Our
advice is limited to the provisions of the Act. We cannot provide you with advice regarding any
other laws that may be applicable to you.
QUESTIONS
1. Does your relationship with the President of the Farmers Lumber Company prevent
the City of Corcoran from continuing to do business with the Farmers Lumber Company?
2. Are you prohibited from participating in decisions by the Corcoran City Council to
purchase materials from the Farmers Lumber Company because of your relationship with the
President of the Farmers Lumber Company?
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; and Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


File No. 1-98-28 1
Page 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. Regardless of your relationship with the president of the Farmers Lumber Company,
the Act would not prohibit the City of Corcoran from continuing to do business with the Farmers
Lumber Company.
2. You are prohibited under the Act from participating in any decision by the city
council, to purchase materials from the Farmers Lumber Company, that would have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on any of your economic interests, that is distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally.
FACTS
You were recently elected to the Corcoran City Council. You live with Allan A. Smith
Jr., the president ofFarmers Lumber Company, Inc., a type S corporation.
The City of Corcoran makes weekly purchases ofmaterials from the Farmers Lumber
Company. The city also issues vouchers to low-income residents ofthe city so that they can
purchase paint from the Farmers Lumber Company, and another vendor, under the city’s
grant-funded Paint Program. None of these purchases are presented to the city council for
individual approval.
You are not an officer or employee of the Farmers Lumber Company, you do not own
any stock in the company, and you do not receive any income from the company.
ANALYSIS
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit a public official from making,
participating in making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which the public official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she
has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) Upon taking office as a member of the Corcoran City
Council, you will become a public official. (Section 82048.)
Whenever a public official has an economic interest in a governmental decision, the
official is required by the Act to abstain from participation in the decision. (Strickland Advice
Letter, No. 1-91-046.) The mere fact that a particular official is required to abstain from
participation in a decision, however, does not mean that any other officials of the same agency,
or the agency as a whole, must also abstain from participation in the decision. The
disqualification requirement imposed by the Act is strictly limited to whichever individual
official or officials have a financial interest in the decision. So even if you have a financial
interest in some decision pending before the city council, because the decision materially affects
the Farmers Lumber Company or Mr. Smith, the city council would still be able to proceed with


File No. 1-98-281
Page 3
the decision on behalf of the City of Corcoran. The decision would simply have to be made
without your participation.
Whether you have a financial interest in a decision is governed by Section 87103, which
provides, in part, that:
“A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his
or her immediate family, or on any of the following:
(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct
or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
more.
(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or
indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a
gift or gifts aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. The
amount of the value of gifts specified by this subdivision shall be
adjusted biennially by the commission to equal the same amount
determined by the commission pursuant to subdivision (±) of
Section 89503g.”
Regulation 18703.5 defines the circumstances under which a decision would be
considered to have a personal financial effect on a public official under Section 87103. This
regulation provides:
At the present time, the amount is $290, and will be $300 as of January 1, 1999.


File No. 1-98-28 1
Page 4
“A governmental decision has a personal financial effect on a
public official if the decision will result in the personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate
family increasing or decreasing. When determining whether a
governmental decision has a personal financial effect on a public
official, a financial effect on the value of real property owned
directly or indirectly by the official, or a financial effect on the
gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a
business entity in which the official has an investment interest shall
not be considered.”
Economic Interests
You have not provided us with any facts indicating that city council decisions involving
the Farmers Lumber Company would have a personal financial effect on you. Similarly, you
have not provided us with any facts indicating that you have an economic interest in either the
Farmers Lumber Company, or Mr. Smith, as described in Section 87103.
Your letter indicates that you have no investment interest in the Farmers Lumber
Company, the company is not a source of income to you, and you hold no position with the
company.
Furthermore, even though you live with Mr. Smith, you do not indicate that
Mr. Smith is a source of income to you (which would include loans), or that you have any legal
right to his earnings. Unless the two of you are married, the mere fact that you live with Mr.
Smith does not give you a legal right to his earnings, and does not make him one of your
economic interests. (Budge Advice Letter, No. 1-93-460.)
As such, unless you have some other economic interest in the Farmers Lumber Company,
or Mr. Smith, under Section 87103, that is not disclosed in your letter, then neither of them
would constitute an economic interest that might require you to disqualify yourself from
participating in city council decisions affecting them.
One aspect of Section 87103 that you did not touch upon in your letter, however, is
whether the Farmers Lumber Company, or Mr. Smith, provide you with any gifts. If either of
them provides you with any gifts totaling $290 or more within a 12-month period prior to a
governmental decision, you will be deemed to have an economic interest in the donor, under
Section 87103(e), unless some exception applies. In the Brown Advice Letter, No. A-96-155, we
concluded that when two people live together, the payment that each of them makes toward their
shared living expenses constitutes a gift from each of them to the other. We also concluded in
the same letter, however, that if the payment of shared living expenses is between individuals
involved in a “bona fide dating relationship,” then the gift falls within a special exception, and
would not give rise to an economic interest under Section 87 103(e).


File No. 1-98-28 1
Page 5
We therefore suggest that you examine the nature of your relationship with the Farmers
Lumber Company, and Mr. Smith, in light of the provisions of Section 87103, to determine for
yourselfwhether either of them constitutes an economic interest for you. Ifneither of them
constitutes an economic interest for you, the Act will not require you to disqualify yourself from
participating in decisions that affect them. If, upon further examination, you find that one or
both of them constitute an economic interest for you, you must conduct a further analysis, as set
forth below, to detenriine if you must disqualify yourself from participating in governmental
decisions affecting the interest.
Reasonably Foreseeable Financial Effect
Determining whether a particular governmental decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on an identified economic interest is essentially a three-step
process.
First, you must determine whether your economic interest will be directly or indirectly
involved in the decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(4).) Regulations 18704.1-18704.5 (copies
enclosed) set forth the standards for determining whether an official’s economic interests are
directly involved in a governmental decision.
Second, you must select the appropriate standard for determining whether the financial
impact of the decision on a particular economic interest will be material. (Regulation
18700(b)(5).) The various materiality standards are set forth in Regulations 18705-18705.5
(copies enclosed). Selection of the appropriate standard is based upon the nature of the economic
interest, and whether the interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision.
Third, you must determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the materiality
standard will be satisfied for any particular economic interest as a consequence of the decision.
(Regulation 18700(b)(6).) A financial effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706.)
If it is substantially likely that the materiality standard will be satisfied for any of your
economic interests, as a consequence of the decision, then you will have a conflict of interest,
and you must not participate in the decision, unless the “public generally exception” applies.
This exception is described in Regulations 18707-18707.6 (copies enclosed).
If it is not substantially likely that the materiality standard will be satisfied for any of
your economic interests, as a consequence of the decision, then you will not have a conflict of
interest under the Act, regarding that particular decision. Evaluating whether or not you have a
conflict of interest in connection with a governmental decision is something that you must do on
a case-by-case basis for each governmental decision in which you participate.


File No. 1-98-28 1
Page 6
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Steven Benito Russo
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does your relationship with the President of the Farmers Lumber Company prevent
the City of Corcoran from continuing to do business with the Farmers Lumber Company?
2. Are you prohibited from participating in decisions by the Corcoran City Council to
purchase materials from the Farmers Lumber Company because of your relationship with the
President of the Farmers Lumber Company?2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; and Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


File No. 1-98-28 1

CONCLUSION: 1. Regardless of your relationship with the president of the Farmers Lumber Company,
the Act would not prohibit the City of Corcoran from continuing to do business with the Farmers
Lumber Company.
2. You are prohibited under the Act from participating in any decision by the city
council, to purchase materials from the Farmers Lumber Company, that would have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on any of your economic interests, that is distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally.
